sections.textAr:
اللقيط: حر مسلم ونفقته من بيت المال

فإن التقطه رجل لم يكن لغيره أن يأخذه من يده فإن ادعى مدع أنه ابنه فالقول قوله وإن ادعاه اثنان ووصف أحدهما علامة في جسده فهو أولى به

وإذا وجد في مصر من أمصار المسلمين أو في قرية من قراهم فادعى ذمي أنه ابنه ثبث نسبه منه وكان مسلما وإن وجد في قرية من قرى أهل الذمة أو في بيعة أو كنيسة كان ذميا

ومن ادعى أن اللقيط عبده لم يقبل منه فإن ادعى عبد أنه ابنه ثبت نسبه منه وكان حرا

وإن وجد مع اللقيط مال مشدود عليه فهو له ولا يجوز تزويج الملتقط ولا تصرفه في مال اللقيط

ويجوز أن يقبض له الهبة ويسلمه في صناعة ويؤاجره

masail.rulingAr:
اللقيط: حر مسلم ونفقته من بيت المال

فإن التقطه رجل لم يكن لغيره أن يأخذه من يده فإن ادعى مدع أنه ابنه فالقول قوله وإن ادعاه اثنان ووصف أحدهما علامة في جسده فهو أولى به

وإذا وجد في مصر من أمصار المسلمين أو في قرية من قراهم فادعى ذمي أنه ابنه ثبث نسبه منه وكان مسلما وإن وجد في قرية من قرى أهل الذمة أو في بيعة أو كنيسة كان ذميا

ومن ادعى أن اللقيط عبده لم يقبل منه فإن ادعى عبد أنه ابنه ثبت نسبه منه وكان حرا

وإن وجد مع اللقيط مال مشدود عليه فهو له ولا يجوز تزويج الملتقط ولا تصرفه في مال اللقيط

ويجوز أن يقبض له الهبة ويسلمه في صناعة ويؤاجره
masail.rulingEn:
A foundling (laqit) is deemed free and Muslim. His maintenance is from the public treasury.

If a man picks him up, no one else may take him from his hands. If a claimant claims he is his son, his word is accepted. If two persons claim him and one of them describes a mark on his body, he has the stronger claim.

If the foundling is found in a city of the Muslims or a village of theirs, and a dhimmi claims he is his son, his lineage is established from him but the child is deemed Muslim. If the foundling is found in a village of the People of the Covenant, or in a church or synagogue, he is deemed a dhimmi.

If someone claims the foundling is his slave, his claim is not accepted. If a slave claims the foundling is his son, his lineage is established from him and the child is free.

If property is found tied to the foundling, it belongs to him. The finder may not marry off the foundling or dispose of his property.

The finder may accept gifts on the foundling's behalf, apprentice him to a trade, and hire him out.

sections.textEn:
A foundling (laqit) is deemed free and Muslim. His maintenance is from the public treasury.

If a man picks him up, no one else may take him from his hands. If a claimant claims he is his son, his word is accepted. If two persons claim him and one of them describes a mark on his body, he has the stronger claim.

If the foundling is found in a city of the Muslims or a village of theirs, and a dhimmi claims he is his son, his lineage is established from him but the child is deemed Muslim. If the foundling is found in a village of the People of the Covenant, or in a church or synagogue, he is deemed a dhimmi.

If someone claims the foundling is his slave, his claim is not accepted. If a slave claims the foundling is his son, his lineage is established from him and the child is free.

If property is found tied to the foundling, it belongs to him. The finder may not marry off the foundling or dispose of his property.

The finder may accept gifts on the foundling's behalf, apprentice him to a trade, and hire him out.

sections.textAr:
اللقطة: أمانة إذا أشهد الملتقط أنه يأخذها ليحفظها ويردها على صاحبها فإن كانت أقل من عشرة دراهم عرفها أياما وإن كانت عشرة فصاعدا عرفها حولا

فإن جاء صاحبها وإلا تصدق بها فإن جاء صاحبها فهو بالخيار: إن شاء أمضى الصدقة وإن شاء ضمن الملتقط

ويجوز الالتقاط في الشاة والبقرة والبعير

فإن أنفق الملتقط عليها بغير إذن الحاكم فهو متبرع وإن أنفق بأمره كان ذلك دينا على مالكها

وإذا رفع ذلك إلى الحاكم نظر فيه فإن كان للبهيمة منفعة أجرها وأنفق عليها من أجرتها وإن لم يكن لها منفعة وخاف أن تستغرق النفقة قيمتها باعها وأمره بحفظ ثمنها وإن كان الأصلح الإنفاق عليها أذن له في ذلك وجعل النفقة دينا على مالكها

فإذا حضر مالكها فللملتقط أن يمنعه منها حتى يأخذ النفقة

ولقطه الحل والحرم سواء

وإذا حضر الرجل فادعى

أن اللقطة له لم تدفع إليه حتى يقم البينة فإن أعطى علامتها حل للملتقط أن يدفعها إليه ولا يجبر على ذلك في القضاء

ولا يتصدق باللقطة على غني وإن كان الملتقط غنيا لم يجز له أن ينتفع بها وإن كان فقيرا فلا بأس أن ينتفع بها ويجوز أن يتصدق بها إذا كان غنيا على أبيه وابنه وزوجته إذا كانوا فقراء والله أعلم

masail.rulingEn:
Found property (luqtah) is a trust if the finder calls witnesses that he takes it to safeguard it and return it to its owner. If it is worth less than ten dirhams, he publicizes it for a few days. If it is ten dirhams or more, he publicizes it for a full year.

If the owner comes, fine; otherwise the finder gives it in charity. If the owner then comes, he has the option: if he wishes, he may affirm the charity; and if he wishes, he may hold the finder liable.

Picking up found sheep, cattle, and camels is permissible.

If the finder spends on the animal without the judge's authorization, he is a volunteer with no recourse. If he spends by the judge's order, it becomes a debt upon the owner.

If the matter is brought before the judge, the judge considers: if the animal can generate revenue, he rents it out and spends on it from its rental income. If it cannot generate revenue and he fears that maintenance costs will consume its value, he sells it and orders the finder to safeguard the proceeds. If keeping the animal is more beneficial, the judge authorizes spending on it and makes the maintenance a debt upon the owner.

When the owner appears, the finder may withhold the animal until he recovers the maintenance expenses.

Found property in the sacred precinct and elsewhere is treated equally.

If a person appears and claims

that the found property is his, it is not given to him until he produces evidence. If he describes its distinguishing features, it is permissible for the finder to give it to him, but he is not compelled to do so by judicial ruling.

The finder may not give the found property in charity to a wealthy person. If the finder is wealthy, he may not benefit from it himself. If the finder is poor, he may benefit from it. A wealthy finder may give it in charity to his father, son, or wife if they are poor. And Allah knows best.
